skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, format, and validate Excel workbooks, CSV files, TSV files, and formula-heavy spreadsheet models.
skill.summary: Build and validate spreadsheet files with formulas, formatting, analysis workflows, and LibreOffice recalculation.
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from documentation examples, spreadsheet error labels, and legal URLs. The confirmed risk is operational: recalc.py runs LibreOffice through subprocess and writes a persistent macro into the LibreOffice user profile. No evidence found of malicious network access, credential theft, prompt injection, obfuscation, or command injection.

medium_findings.title: LibreOffice Subprocess Execution
medium_findings.description: recalc.py runs soffice and timeout-related commands with subprocess.run. The calls use argument arrays rather than shell strings, so command injection evidence was not found, but the skill still launches external programs against user-provided workbook paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are real and semantically required for formula recalculation. Confidence is not higher because arguments are not passed through a shell and no malicious command construction was found.

medium_findings.title: Persistent LibreOffice Macro Installation
medium_findings.description: recalc.py creates a LibreOffice macro directory under the user's profile and writes Module1.xba. The macro content appears limited to recalculating, saving, and closing the active workbook, but persistent profile modification should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write into a hidden LibreOffice profile path is directly visible and persistent. The macro body is simple and not malicious, which keeps the severity at medium.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Non-Operational
low_findings.description: The URLs in LICENSE.txt are Anthropic legal terms, and the URL in recalc.py is an OpenOffice XML namespace inside macro XML. No HTTP client or outbound request behavior was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are legal text and XML metadata, not executable network code. No network library usage or data transmission was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger External Command Flags
low_findings.description: Most SKILL.md external-command detections are code blocks and usage instructions for pandas, openpyxl, recalc.py, and spreadsheet validation. They are not runtime code executed by the skill marketplace package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged material is prose or fenced examples, not executable package code. The only confirmed executable command behavior is separately captured in recalc.py.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Cryptography Flags Are Spreadsheet Text
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword flag maps to spreadsheet cell references in formulas, and weak-cryptography flags map to ordinary prose or code examples. No encryption, malware command-and-control, or obfuscated payload behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are spreadsheet instructions and formula examples. The reviewed files contain no crypto operations or network beacon logic.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Flags Are Spreadsheet Guidance
low_findings.description: The cited lines discuss spreadsheet error codes, Excel data type handling, and concise generated Python style. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment probing, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line content is visible spreadsheet workflow guidance. The only platform checks found are used to choose LibreOffice profile paths and timeout command behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Process Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a helper script that invokes LibreOffice and timeout utilities. This is expected for recalculation but should run only on trusted workbook files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and reachable during normal use. No shell interpolation was found, reducing command injection concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes to Hidden Application Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script writes a macro file under the hidden LibreOffice user configuration directory. This is functional setup behavior but persists outside the target workbook.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The profile path and write operation are directly present. The macro content is transparent and limited to recalculation operations.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often needs formulas, formatting, and validation across complex files. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to build, edit, analyze, and recalculate Excel workbooks with fewer formula errors.
actual_capabilities: Creates new Excel workbooks with formulas, formatting, sheets, and structured tables.
Reads and analyzes spreadsheet data with pandas workflows for Excel, CSV, and TSV files.
Edits existing workbooks while preserving formulas and formatting through openpyxl guidance.
Recalculates workbook formulas with LibreOffice using the included recalc.py helper script.
Checks recalculated files for common Excel formula errors and reports affected locations.
Applies financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and key assumptions.
limitations: Formula recalculation depends on LibreOffice being installed and available as soffice.
The helper script modifies the local LibreOffice user profile to install a macro.
It does not replace human review for financial model logic or business assumptions.
Very large workbooks may take longer to read, recalculate, or scan for errors.

use_cases.title: Build a Financial Model
use_cases.description: Create an Excel model with assumptions, linked formulas, formatting conventions, and formula error checks.

use_cases.title: Clean and Analyze Spreadsheet Data
use_cases.description: Load CSV or Excel data, inspect columns, summarize values, and export a clean workbook.

use_cases.title: Update an Existing Template
use_cases.description: Modify workbook data while preserving existing sheet structure, formulas, styles, and template conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this data. Add clear headers, basic formatting, totals, and a summary sheet.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Uploaded Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this spreadsheet. Summarize each sheet, identify data quality issues, and create a cleaned Excel output.

prompt_templates.title: Update a Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this Excel template with the new data. Preserve formatting, formulas, sheet names, and existing layout conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Formula Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this workbook for formula consistency. Recalculate formulas, identify Excel errors, and explain the cells that need correction.

output_examples.input: Create a revenue tracker with monthly sales, expenses, gross margin, and year-to-date totals.
output_examples.output: A formatted Excel workbook with monthly input rows, linked formulas, margin calculations, and a summary section.

output_examples.input: Analyze this uploaded sales spreadsheet and identify missing values or unusual totals.
output: A concise data quality summary by worksheet.
A list of columns with missing or inconsistent values.
A cleaned workbook ready for review.

output_examples.input: Recalculate this workbook and check whether any formulas fail.
output_examples.output: A validation report showing the recalculation status, formula error types, and affected worksheet cells.

best_practices: Use formulas for calculated workbook values so users can update assumptions later.
Recalculate and scan formula-heavy workbooks before delivering final files.
Preserve existing workbook style and structure when editing user-provided templates.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode calculated results when an Excel formula should remain dynamic.
Do not save workbooks opened with data-only mode when formulas must be preserved.
Do not run recalculation on untrusted workbooks without reviewing the file source.

faq.question: What file types does this skill support?
faq.answer: It focuses on Excel workbooks and tabular files, including .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, and .tsv.

faq.question: Can it preserve existing workbook formulas?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance uses openpyxl workflows that can load, edit, and save workbooks while preserving formulas.

faq.question: Does formula recalculation require extra software?
faq.answer: Yes. The included recalculation workflow expects LibreOffice to be installed and available from the command line.

faq.question: Can it find formula errors?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper script scans recalculated workbooks for common Excel errors such as reference, value, and division errors.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it runs LibreOffice and writes a local LibreOffice macro.

faq.question: Is it suitable for financial models?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes conventions for assumptions, formula construction, formatting, and validation of spreadsheet models.
